nodes.label: ripgrep
nodes.description: Line-oriented search tool that recursively searches the current directory for a regex pattern. Binary is named rg. First-class on Windows, macOS, Linux. Written in Rust, dual-licensed MIT / UNLICENSE.

nodes.label: Speed
nodes.description: Core property of ripgrep. Across canonical benchmarks (Linux kernel source for [A-Z]+_SUSPEND, 13GB OpenSubtitles, deep directory traversal), ripgrep is the fastest tool except in rare edge cases. Speed is the central selling point alongside default filtering.

nodes.label: Rust regex engine
nodes.description: The crate rust-lang/regex powering ripgrep's default search. Uses finite automata (DFA), SIMD, and aggressive literal-prefix optimisations. Not a backtracking engine — provides O(n) worst-case guarantees.

nodes.label: Finite automata
nodes.description: Regex implementation strategy that compiles the pattern to a deterministic state machine traversed linearly over the input. Avoids the catastrophic backtracking of NFA/backref-style engines.

nodes.label: SIMD literal optimization
nodes.description: Use of SIMD (vector) instructions to skim large input chunks for fixed-literal substrings extracted from the regex prefix. Skips entire regions where no literal can match, then runs the slower automaton only on candidate regions.

nodes.label: UTF-8 in DFA
nodes.description: Rust regex builds UTF-8 decoding directly into its DFA states rather than decoding then matching. Lets Unicode-aware patterns run at full speed, unlike GNU grep which slows ~30x with LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8.

nodes.label: Memory map strategy
nodes.description: For single large files, ripgrep mmaps the file and lets the OS page in chunks on demand. Lower setup cost than reading into a buffer, optimal for one-file searches.

nodes.label: Incremental buffer strategy
nodes.description: For directory walks, ripgrep reads each file in chunks into a reusable buffer. Avoids mmap overhead per file when there are many small files.

nodes.label: Automatic strategy selection
nodes.description: ripgrep picks between mmap and incremental-buffer based on whether the search target is a single file or a directory tree. The user does not configure this — it's a default heuristic chosen to minimize total search time.

nodes.label: Gitignore filtering
nodes.description: By default ripgrep parses .gitignore, .ignore, and .rgignore files and excludes matching paths from the search. Implemented atop a RegexSet so a path is matched against all patterns at once.

nodes.label: RegexSet ignore matching
nodes.description: Single compiled set of regexes that can match an input string against all member patterns simultaneously. Used by ripgrep's ignore crate to test a path against every gitignore line in one pass rather than N times.

nodes.label: Hidden file skip
nodes.description: By default ripgrep skips dotfiles and dot-directories. Like gitignore filtering, can be disabled with -uuu.

nodes.label: Binary file skip
nodes.description: By default ripgrep detects binary files and excludes them from output. Prevents the user from getting paged through millions of garbage match lines from compiled artifacts.

nodes.label: Parallel directory walk
nodes.description: Lock-free parallel recursive directory iterator implemented with the crossbeam and ignore crates. Multiple worker threads consume directory entries from a shared queue, each searching its files concurrently.

nodes.label: crossbeam
nodes.description: Rust concurrency-primitives crate providing the lock-free channels and work-stealing utilities ripgrep uses to coordinate worker threads.

nodes.label: ignore crate
nodes.description: Standalone Rust crate (also maintained by ripgrep's author) that implements gitignore semantics and the parallel directory iterator. Reused by other tools beyond ripgrep.

nodes.label: Default Unicode
nodes.description: Unicode mode is always on by default in ripgrep, in contrast to GNU grep where Unicode requires LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 and costs ~30x slowdown.

nodes.label: PCRE2 support
nodes.description: Optional second regex engine accessible via -P / --pcre2. Adds look-around assertions and backreferences not available in the default Rust regex engine. Built in via the pcre2 cargo feature; auto-discovers system PCRE2 or builds it from source.

nodes.label: Look-around assertion
nodes.description: Regex construct (?=...) (?<=...) (?!...) (?<!...) that matches based on surrounding context without consuming characters. Not expressible in finite-automata engines; needs a backtracking engine like PCRE2.

nodes.label: Backreference
nodes.description: Regex construct that matches the same text captured by a previous group (\1, \2...). Forces a backtracking implementation and breaks the O(n) guarantee of finite automata.

nodes.label: File-type filtering
nodes.description: Flags -t/-T let the user restrict the search to specific file types (rg -tpy matches only Python). Types can be extended at runtime with custom matching rules.

nodes.label: Multiline search
nodes.description: Optional mode where the regex can match across line boundaries. Off by default because it changes the line-oriented semantics that anchor the rest of the tool.

nodes.label: Text encoding support
nodes.description: Beyond UTF-8, ripgrep can read UTF-16, latin-1, GBK, EUC-JP, Shift_JIS and others via -E. Autodetects UTF-16; other encodings need explicit selection.

nodes.label: Compressed file search
nodes.description: Flag -z lets ripgrep transparently decompress brotli, bzip2, gzip, lz4, lzma, xz and zstandard before searching. Costs setup time but avoids manual zcat | grep pipelines.

nodes.label: Preprocessor support
nodes.description: Arbitrary external command can be run on each file before searching its output. Enables PDF text extraction, unusual decompression, decryption, encoding detection — anything that produces text on stdout.

nodes.label: Configuration file
nodes.description: ripgrep reads default flags from a file pointed to by $RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH. Lets the user encode preferred defaults (smart-case, hidden files, type aliases) once.

nodes.label: POSIX non-conformance
nodes.description: ripgrep does not implement any standard. Flag conventions and output format are pragmatic, not POSIX-spec'd. Means ripgrep cannot be a drop-in replacement in scripts that depend on grep's exact behaviour.

nodes.label: Ubiquitous tool requirement
nodes.description: Use case where the user needs the search tool to be present on every UNIX-like system out of the box. ripgrep is not pre-installed anywhere; for this case good old grep is the answer.

nodes.label: Feature creep tension
nodes.description: Initial design philosophy of ripgrep was minimalism, avoiding 'every feature under the sun'. Over time multiline search, PCRE2 and many others were added because the real-world demand existed. Author acknowledges the evolution rather than denying it.

nodes.label: simd-accel removal
nodes.description: ripgrep used to support a simd-accel cargo feature requiring nightly Rust, benefiting only UTF-16 transcoding via encoding_rs. Removed because nightly-only features kept breaking the build. UTF-16 SIMD now blocked on encoding_rs adopting stable APIs.

nodes.label: Snap removal
nodes.description: Snap packages for Ubuntu were officially deprecated by the author. Bug reports filed against them were unreproducible and frequent; maintainer time was finite. Recommended alternative: the .deb file from each release.

rels.evidence: The Rust regex engine's combination of finite automata, SIMD-accelerated literal search, and aggressive prefix-literal optimization is the primary reason ripgrep is fast — without that engine, the rest of ripgrep would be a pretty wrapper.
DFAs are the matching engine inside the Rust regex crate.
SIMD literal skim is a layer the Rust regex engine runs before the DFA to avoid touching unpromising regions.
UTF-8 decoding states are compiled into the DFA itself rather than handled in a separate pre-pass.
Because UTF-8 awareness is built into the DFA at zero per-step cost, ripgrep can keep Unicode on by default without paying GNU-grep's ~30x slowdown — Unicode becomes free.
Mmap eliminates copy-from-kernel overhead for single-file searches by letting the OS page in only the bytes actually touched by the scanner.
Reusing a small buffer per file avoids the per-file mmap setup syscall that dominates when traversing many small files in a directory.
The auto-selector dispatches to mmap for single files.
The auto-selector dispatches to the incremental buffer for directory walks.

By picking the right I/O strategy for each workload without user input, ripgrep avoids the common pitfall of forcing one strategy on all cases — this is what makes the default fast on both single-file and tree workloads.
Gitignore filtering is implemented on top of a single RegexSet built from all ignore patterns.
Matching a path against N gitignore patterns in one DFA traversal instead of N separate matches is what keeps directory walks fast in deeply-ignored trees.
Both are 'safe defaults' filters that hide noise; they share the same -uuu disable mechanism.
Hidden-file skip is the second of three safe-defaults filters; same -uuu disable mechanism.
Lock-free parallel walking lets multiple cores search disjoint subtrees simultaneously, scaling near-linearly with core count on cold caches.
Parallel walk uses crossbeam channels and work-stealing primitives — without them the implementation would have to roll its own concurrency layer.
The ignore crate provides both the gitignore engine and the parallel iterator — they ship together.
The ignore crate is the actual code path that parses and applies gitignore-style files. ripgrep is a consumer.
Look-around is impossible to express in the default DFA engine; the PCRE2 opt-in is the only way to get it in ripgrep.
Backrefs require backtracking which the default engine refuses to implement on principle; PCRE2 provides them.
Backreferences force backtracking which breaks the O(n) worst-case guarantee finite automata provide — they are fundamentally incompatible matching models.
General look-around cannot be expressed as a finite-state machine; the two formalisms diverge once the regex needs to inspect surrounding context without consuming it.
If a user's use case requires a tool present on every UNIX out of the box, ripgrep cannot satisfy it — POSIX grep can. The README states this trade-off explicitly.
Because the Unicode tax is paid at DFA-construction time rather than per byte, keeping Unicode default does not compromise the speed property.
Gitignore filtering is the headline default-on filter.
Binary skip is one of the three default-on filters that distinguish ripgrep from plain grep.
Hidden-file skip is the third of the default-on filters.
File-type filtering is exposed via -t / -T and is documented in the user guide.
Opt-in multiline support is one of the late additions documented in the recent-features paragraph.
Non-UTF-8 encoding handling sits in ripgrep and uses encoding_rs.
-z gating compressed-file search ships in the binary.
Preprocessor flag is documented and is the official escape hatch for unusual input formats.
Config-file mechanism via $RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH is documented in the guide.
Multiline search is one of the late additions that the README's 'Why shouldn't I use ripgrep' paragraph acknowledges, breaking the original minimalism stance.
PCRE2 opt-in is explicitly cited as a feature added despite the initial minimalist intent — user demand for look-around and backref outweighed the design purity argument.
Removing simd-accel reduced UTF-16 transcoding speed; ripgrep still supports UTF-16 functionally but without the SIMD path it used to have, until encoding_rs stabilises its API.

Killing the Ubuntu snap removed one of the easy install paths on a popular distro, pushing users to the .deb file or apt — slight regression on ubiquity, intentional because of un-debuggable bug reports.
The Rust regex crate is the single most important dependency; ripgrep is essentially a CLI wrapper around it with directory traversal and ignore-file logic on top.
Parallel walk is the second pillar of the binary, on equal footing with the regex engine.
